original_abstract:
This article explores the theological foundations of hope within the context of pastoral care (*cura animarum*), specifically examining the transformative potential of a theology of the resurrection (*theologia resurrectionis*). The author begins by addressing critical existential questions: is hope merely a psychological defense mechanism against despair and dread, or does it offer a substantive framework for navigating suffering? Central to this inquiry is the distinction between hope as a superficial emotional state and hope as a qualitative spiritual category that describes the "courage to be." 

Building on the work of thinkers like Søren Kierkegaard, Gabriel Marcel, and Jürgen Moltmann, the paper argues that resurrection hope functions as a radical paradox—reframing the finality of death and the paralysis of "nothingness." The author contends that traditional Western theology has often marginalized the resurrection in favor of the cross, resulting in either a somber focus on atonement or a "cheap triumphantalism" (*theologia gloriae*). In contrast, this research proposes that the resurrection offers a "thickened" alternative story for human existence, defining identity not by social or biological categories, but by an eschatological "new creation." 

The paper concludes that pastoral caregiving, when rooted in resurrection theology, becomes "hope care." This approach does not bypass the reality of pain but empowers individuals to find meaning and dignity within it. By shifting the perspective from *futurum* (forecasting) to *parousia* (the presence of the new being), the resurrection provides a grounded ontology that enables resistance against dehumanizing discourses and fosters a sustainable orientation toward life.
humanized_abstract:
This article on the theology of hope in pastoral care deals with the influence of a theology of the resurrection. It initially deals with the existential questions to which almost all people seek an answer: What is hope really? Is it a defensive reaction against feelings of hopelessness and dread, or is it something more substantial that can provide a meaningful and adequate approach to suffering? Here we have to do with a fundamental difference between the psychological and superficial sense of hope and its qualitative, spiritual meaning, which provides the “courage to be”.

Drawing on the thought of Søren Kierkegaard, Gabriel Marcel and Jürgen Moltmann, this paper argues that resurrection hope presents a radical paradox that recontextualises death as a finality and “nothingness” as a state of utter helplessness. The paper further argues that Western theology has often marginalised the resurrection of Jesus in favour of the cross, either focusing solely on the atonement and thereby presenting a “dark” message, or reducing the gospel to a “cheap triumphantalism” (*theologia gloriae*). Rather than presenting a thin alternative narrative to the social and biological identity with which we are used to live, the resurrection offers a “thickened” alternative narrative in which humanity is defined through an eschatological new creation.

According to the paper, pastoral care can become “hope care” when based in resurrection theology. The authors argue that this form of care “does not circumvent pain but gives us the space to make sense of it and to honour the humanity in it and it in us”. With a detailed explanation of how the resurrection affords a “ontological basis that supports our fight against reducing human existence to reductionist discourses” and provides a foundation for a life-affirming orientation that is not depleted. The authors use philosophical terminology to describe this shift, arguing that the resurrection allows us to leave behind a focus on the future (forecasts) and instead look toward the parousia – “the presence of the new being”.
